question: Why are discriminated unions with _tag better than class inheritance for error hierarchies?

options.text: Discriminated unions provide exhaustive checking in switch — the compiler guarantees all error types are handled
Classes don't support inheritance in TypeScript
options.explanation: TypeScript fully supports classes and inheritance. The issue isn't support, but that instanceof checks are less reliable than discriminated unions for exhaustive checking

options.text: Discriminated unions perform faster at runtime
options.explanation: Performance is comparable. The advantage is type safety: switch on _tag provides exhaustive check, while instanceof doesn't guarantee complete handling

options.text: Error classes can't be serialized to JSON
options.explanation: Classes can be serialized (by implementing toJSON). The main advantage of unions is that TypeScript automatically narrows types in switch and flags incomplete handling

question: What does an Error Boundary do in the context of type-safe architecture?

options.text: Converts unknown from catch into a specific typed error type, isolating the layer's error domain
Suppresses all errors and returns null
options.explanation: A boundary doesn't suppress errors. It converts unknown into a typed error and returns Result<T, E>, where the error is explicitly present in the type

options.text: Automatically retries the operation on error
options.explanation: Retrying is a retry strategy, not a boundary. A boundary is responsible for mapping unknown -> typed error and returning Result

options.text: Logs errors and rethrows them via throw
options.explanation: A boundary doesn't rethrow errors via throw. It returns Result<T, E>, turning exceptions into values and eliminating the need for try/catch above

question: How does flatMap in ResultChain accumulate error types?

options.text: Through type union: flatMap<U, E2> returns ResultChain<U, E | E2>, combining errors from the current and next operation
Through an error array: each error is added to a list
options.explanation: Errors accumulate at the type level through union (|), not at runtime through an array. The actual error is always one — the one that occurred

options.text: Through type intersection: E & E2
options.explanation: Intersection (&) means the error must have ALL properties of both types. Union (|) means the error can be ANY of the types — this is the correct semantics

options.text: flatMap doesn't accumulate errors — each call replaces the error type
options.explanation: flatMap specifically accumulates: if the first operation can produce E1 and the second E2, the result has type E1 | E2. This is a key property of Result chains

question: What states can a Circuit Breaker be in?

options.text: Closed — normal operation, requests pass through
Open — after exceeding failure threshold, requests are blocked
Half-open — trial mode after timeout, one request passes for verification
Degraded — partial operation with limited functionality
options.explanation: Degraded is not a standard Circuit Breaker state. The classic model includes only three states: closed, open, half-open

question: Why does withFallback return Result<T, { primary: E1; fallback: E2 }> instead of Result<T, E1 | E2>?

options.text: To preserve information about which specific error occurred in primary and which in fallback — this is important for diagnostics
Because TypeScript doesn't support union in Result
options.explanation: TypeScript supports union in Result perfectly. The { primary, fallback } structure is chosen for informativeness — we know both errors, not just one of two

options.text: For memory savings by storing only one object
options.explanation: An object with two fields uses more memory than a union. The reason is diagnostic value: when debugging, both errors are visible

options.text: So you can retry only the failed operation
options.explanation: Both operations failed if we reached this error. The { primary, fallback } structure is needed for full diagnostics: what went wrong in each strategy
